schools.summary: The judgment of the great whore: 'and there came one of the seven angels which had the seven vials, and talked with me, saying unto me, Come hither; I will shew unto thee the judgment of the great whore that sitteth upon many waters' — one of the vial-angels inviting John to see the judgment of 'the great whore' (the vision now elaborating the fall of Babylon, 16:19, by portraying her as a great harlot under judgment); 'the great whore (harlot)' the symbolic figure of the great corrupt power, Babylon (v.5), the harlot-image marking her spiritual fornication (idolatry, false worship, and corrupting alliance with the world, the historicists reading her as papal Rome/the apostate church, others as pagan Rome, the figure of unfaithfulness to God); 'that sitteth upon many waters' explained later (v.15) as peoples, multitudes, nations, and tongues (she sitting enthroned over many peoples, her dominion and influence extending over the nations) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill, Ellicott.

Victorinus and Bede: the great whore who sits upon many waters is the great city/power that rules over the nations and peoples; the harlot enthroned over the multitudes signifies the corrupt world-power (Babylon) in its dominion and seduction of the nations, whose judgment the angel now unfolds — the unfaithful city set over against the faithful bride, the New Jerusalem.

themes: Come hither; I will shew unto thee the judgment of the great whore that sitteth upon many waters
the angel offering to show the judgment of the great whore
the corrupt power Babylon enthroned over the peoples
the great harlot brought to judgment

schools.summary: The whore's seduction: 'with whom the kings of the earth have committed fornication, and the inhabitants of the earth have been made drunk with the wine of her fornication' — the kings of the earth having committed fornication with the whore, and the earth-dwellers made drunk with the wine of her fornication (her corrupting influence having seduced both rulers and peoples into her idolatry and false worship); 'the kings of the earth have committed fornication' (the rulers entering into corrupt alliance with the harlot-power, sharing her idolatry and worldly corruption, the spiritual fornication of the kings with Babylon); 'the inhabitants of the earth have been made drunk with the wine of her fornication' (the peoples intoxicated, stupefied, and seduced by her corrupting influence, 14:8, her false worship and luxury making the nations drunk, the universal seduction of the world by the harlot-power) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: with whom the kings of the earth have committed fornication
the inhabitants of the earth have been made drunk with the wine of her fornication
the kings and earth-dwellers corrupted by the whore
rulers and peoples seduced by the harlot-power

schools.summary: The woman on the scarlet beast: 'so he carried me away in the spirit into the wilderness: and I saw a woman sit upon a scarlet coloured beast, full of names of blasphemy, having seven heads and ten horns' — John carried in the Spirit to a wilderness, seeing a woman (the harlot) seated on a scarlet beast (the woman's sitting on the beast marking her alliance with and support by the persecuting power, the beast of ch. 13, the scarlet color marking luxury and bloodshed); the 'scarlet coloured beast, full of names of blasphemy' the same beast as ch. 13 (the persecuting world-power, scarlet marking its imperial pomp and blood-guilt, 'full of names of blasphemy' marking its blasphemous titles and claims, the harlot riding and upheld by this blasphemous power); 'having seven heads and ten horns' (as 13:1; 12:3) the symbols explained later (vv.9-12) as seven mountains/kings and ten kings (the harlot supported by the beast with its heads and horns, the antichristian world-power) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: I saw a woman sit upon a scarlet coloured beast, full of names of blasphemy, having seven heads and ten horns
the woman seated on the scarlet beast
the harlot upheld by the blasphemous, persecuting world-power
the corrupt power carried by the beast

schools.summary: The whore's array and cup: 'and the woman was arrayed in purple and scarlet colour, and decked with gold and precious stones and pearls, having a golden cup in her hand full of abominations and filthiness of her fornication' — the whore clothed in royal and costly colors (purple and scarlet) and adorned with gold, jewels, and pearls (the figure of worldly pomp, luxury, wealth, and splendor, the seductive magnificence of the harlot-power, in the historicist reading the pomp and wealth of corrupt Rome, the costly array her allurement and worldly glory, decked to seduce, the outward magnificence concealing inward corruption); 'a golden cup in her hand full of abominations and filthiness of her fornication' (the golden cup, outwardly attractive, full of abominations and the filth of her fornication, the contrast between the golden vessel and its foul contents marking the hypocrisy of the harlot — fair without, corrupt within — the cup with which she intoxicates the nations, vv.2; Jeremiah 51:7) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the woman was arrayed in purple and scarlet colour, and decked with gold and precious stones and pearls
having a golden cup in her hand full of abominations and filthiness of her fornication
the whore's luxurious array and the golden cup of abominations
splendid without, corrupt within

schools.summary: The name and identity: 'and upon her forehead was a name written, MYSTERY, BABYLON THE GREAT, THE MOTHER OF HARLOTS AND ABOMINATIONS OF THE EARTH' — the harlot bearing a name on her forehead (as harlots wore their names); 'MYSTERY' marking the name as symbolic, to be understood spiritually not literally; 'BABYLON THE GREAT' identifying her with the great corrupt power. Barnes (with Gill) reads 'Babylon the Great' as papal Rome — 'the nominal head of the Christian world, as Babylon had been of the heathen world,' the apostate church, the corrupt religious power seated on seven hills (v.9), the historicist identification; the name 'Babylon' marking her as the seat of idolatry, corruption, and opposition to God (as ancient Babylon); 'THE MOTHER OF HARLOTS AND ABOMINATIONS OF THE EARTH' her title (the source and chief of all spiritual fornication and abomination, the fountainhead of idolatry and corruption from which other harlotries spring, the mother of the earth's spiritual whoredoms).

debates.question: Who is 'MYSTERY, BABYLON THE GREAT,' the great whore?

sides.position: Papal Rome / the apostate church — the corrupt religious power seated on seven hills (historicist)
Pagan Rome — the persecuting imperial city (preterist)
The apostate world-system or false religion in general

themes: upon her forehead was a name written, MYSTERY, BABYLON THE GREAT
THE MOTHER OF HARLOTS AND ABOMINATIONS OF THE EARTH
the name on the whore's forehead
the crux of Babylon's identity (papal or pagan Rome)

schools.summary: The whore's bloodguilt: 'and I saw the woman drunken with the blood of the saints, and with the blood of the martyrs of Jesus: and when I saw her, I wondered with great admiration' — the whore drunk with the blood of the saints and martyrs (she having shed so much of their blood that she is intoxicated with it, the figure of a great persecuting power guilty of the blood of God's people, in the historicist reading the persecutions of corrupt Rome); 'the blood of the saints, and... the martyrs of Jesus' her crime (those put to death for their faith, her bloodguilt the killing of the faithful witnesses of Christ, the great persecutor of the church); 'when I saw her, I wondered with great admiration (thauma — amazement)' John greatly astonished at the sight (whether amazed at her splendor, her bloodguilt, or the mystery of the vision, his wonder occasioning the angel's explanation, v.7, the astonishing spectacle of the blood-drunk harlot in splendor) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

themes: I saw the woman drunken with the blood of the saints, and with the blood of the martyrs of Jesus
when I saw her, I wondered with great admiration
the whore drunk with the blood of the saints
the splendid harlot guilty of the martyrs' blood

schools.summary: The angel's promised explanation: 'and the angel said unto me, Wherefore didst thou marvel? I will tell thee the mystery of the woman, and of the beast that carrieth her, which hath the seven heads and ten horns' — the angel, seeing John's amazement (v.6), promising to explain the mystery (the hidden, symbolic meaning) of the woman and the beast that carries her (the interpretation of the vision following, vv.8-18); 'the mystery of the woman (the harlot) and of the beast that carrieth her' both the harlot and the beast that supports her to be explained (the symbolic meaning of the seven heads and ten horns now disclosed); 'which hath the seven heads and ten horns' the symbols whose meaning the angel now explains (vv.9-12): the seven heads (mountains/kings) and the ten horns (kings), the explanation of the beast's features unlocking the vision — Barnes, Robertson, Henry.

themes: I will tell thee the mystery of the woman, and of the beast that carrieth her, which hath the seven heads and ten horns
the angel's offer to explain the mystery of the woman and beast
the symbolic vision now to be unfolded
both harlot and beast to be explained

schools.summary: The beast that was, and is not: 'the beast that thou sawest was, and is not; and shall ascend out of the bottomless pit, and go into perdition: and they that dwell on the earth shall wonder, whose names were not written in the book of life from the foundation of the world, when they behold the beast that was, and is not, and yet is' — the beast described as one that 'was, and is not, and yet is/shall ascend' (it existed, then seemed to pass away, then rises again from the abyss, only to go into destruction); interpreters applying it to the fall and revival of the persecuting power (13:3, the preterist reading the Nero-redivivus expectation, the historicist the decline and revival of the antichristian power, its end perdition); 'was, and is not, and yet is' the paradoxical existence (it had been, then was not in some sense fallen or eclipsed, yet is/will be again, the mysterious survival and revival of the persecuting power, doomed at last to perdition); 'they that dwell on the earth shall wonder, whose names were not written in the book of life from the foundation of the world' (the unregenerate world, not written in the book of life, marveling at the beast's revival, 13:3, 8, only the elect not deceived) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill.

Cyril: the earth-dwellers whose names are not in the book of life wonder after the beast that was, and is not, and yet is — the unregenerate world deceived by the apparent revival of the persecuting power, while the elect, written in the book of life, are kept from the deception; the beast's mysterious survival ending in perdition, its doom certain despite the world's wondering allegiance.

themes: the beast that thou sawest was, and is not; and shall ascend out of the bottomless pit, and go into perdition
they that dwell on the earth shall wonder... when they behold the beast that was, and is not, and yet is
the beast that was, and is not, and yet is
the fallen and revived persecuting power, doomed to perdition

schools.summary: The seven heads as seven mountains: 'and here is the mind which hath wisdom. The seven heads are seven mountains, on which the woman sitteth' — the angel inviting discerning wisdom (as 13:18) and explaining the seven heads as 'seven mountains, on which the woman sitteth' (the seven mountains/hills on which the harlot is enthroned); 'here is the mind which hath wisdom' inviting the wise to understand the symbol (the explanation requiring spiritual discernment to apply rightly); 'the seven heads are seven mountains, on which the woman sitteth' almost universally taken as the seven hills of Rome (the city famously built on seven hills, the harlot enthroned on seven mountains pointing unmistakably to Rome, the historicists, preterists, and most interpreters agreeing the seven mountains identify the city as Rome, the heads also signifying seven kings, v.10) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: here is the mind which hath wisdom. The seven heads are seven mountains, on which the woman sitteth
the seven heads as seven mountains
the seven hills of Rome identifying the harlot's seat
the call for spiritual discernment

schools.summary: The seven kings as forms of Roman government (historicist): 'and there are seven kings: five are fallen, and one is, and the other is not yet come; and when he cometh, he must continue a short space' — Barnes reads the seven kings/heads as seven successive forms of Roman government or dynasties ('dynasties or forms of government that had ceased to be' — kings, consuls, dictators, decemvirs, military tribunes, emperors, etc.); 'five are fallen' (past forms), 'one is' (the form then existing), 'the other is not yet come' (a form yet future); the sequence placing the time of writing at the sixth head (the then-reigning power), the seventh king/form when it comes lasting only 'a short space' (transient, giving way to the eighth, the beast, v.11); the seven representing a succession of ruling powers — Barnes, Ellicott, Wesley (who reads seven supreme forms of government).

The seven kings as Roman emperors (preterist): the seven heads are seven individual Roman emperors — counting from Augustus (or Julius Caesar): 'five are fallen' (the dead emperors), 'one is' (the emperor reigning at the time of writing), 'the other is not yet come' (the next emperor, to continue a short space); this reading dates the book by identifying the sixth emperor, and takes the eighth (v.11) as a returning emperor (Nero redivivus). Gill notes that neither seven ages of the church nor simply seven emperors are intended on his own view; the chief division is between forms of government (Barnes, historicist) and individual emperors (preterist).

debates.question: What are the 'seven kings' (five fallen, one is, one to come)?

sides.position: Seven successive forms or dynasties of Roman government (kings, consuls, emperors, etc.) — historicist
Seven individual Roman emperors, counted from Augustus/Julius (the sixth reigning at the time of writing) — preterist

themes: there are seven kings: five are fallen, and one is, and the other is not yet come
when he cometh, he must continue a short space
the seven kings
the crux of their meaning (forms of government vs Roman emperors)

schools.summary: The beast as the eighth: 'and the beast that was, and is not, even he is the eighth, and is of the seven, and goeth into perdition' — the beast (the persecuting power, v.8) being the 'eighth' king, yet 'of the seven' (springing from them, of the same kind), a continuation and culmination of the seven yet distinct as the eighth, its end (like the beast's, v.8) perdition; 'the eighth, and is of the seven' the paradox (both an eighth power and one of the seven, the revived beast — the persecuting power in its final or revived form — reckoned as an eighth yet belonging to the same series, the preterist reading a returning emperor, Nero redivivus, as the eighth, the historicist the final form of the antichristian power); 'goeth into perdition' the doom (its end destruction, however it revives or culminates the beast doomed to perish, the certain overthrow of the persecuting power, the repetition of v.8 stressing its appointed ruin) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the beast that was, and is not, even he is the eighth, and is of the seven, and goeth into perdition
the beast as the eighth king, of the seven
the revived antichristian power, both eighth and of the series
doomed to destruction

schools.summary: The ten horns as ten kings: 'and the ten horns which thou sawest are ten kings, which have received no kingdom as yet; but receive power as kings one hour with the beast' — the ten horns ten kings who had not yet received their kingdom (at the time of the vision), but receive royal power 'one hour' (for a short time) together with the beast (the kings allied with and subject to the beast for a brief period); 'ten kings, which have received no kingdom as yet' (yet future when John wrote, in the historicist reading the kingdoms/powers that arose out of the divided Roman empire, the ten kingdoms of the broken empire, rising to share power with the beast); 'receive power as kings one hour with the beast' (their authority brief and exercised in alliance with the beast, they giving their power to the beast, v.13, and warring with the Lamb, v.14, the short-lived confederacy of kings supporting the antichristian power) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: the ten horns which thou sawest are ten kings, which have received no kingdom as yet
but receive power as kings one hour with the beast
the ten horns as ten kings allied with the beast
the short-lived confederacy supporting the antichristian power

schools.summary: The ten kings' united support: 'these have one mind, and shall give their power and strength unto the beast' — the ten kings of one mind (united in purpose) and giving their power and strength to the beast (placing their authority and resources at the service of the antichristian power, the united support of the kings for the beast); 'these have one mind' their unity (agreed, united in a common purpose to support the beast, their concord serving the antichristian cause); 'shall give their power and strength unto the beast' the surrendered authority (the kings surrendering their authority and might to the beast, becoming its instruments, the concentration of the kings' power in the beast for the war against the Lamb, v.14, the willing subordination of the kings to the antichristian power) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: these have one mind, and shall give their power and strength unto the beast
the ten kings giving their power to the beast
the united confederacy surrendering authority to the beast
the kings becoming the beast's instruments

schools.summary: The war with the Lamb and his victory: 'these shall make war with the Lamb, and the Lamb shall overcome them: for he is Lord of lords, and King of kings: and they that are with him are called, and chosen, and faithful' — the beast and the ten kings making war against the Lamb (Christ), but the Lamb overcoming them (the climactic conflict between the antichristian powers and Christ ending in Christ's victory, the assured triumph of the Lamb over all his enemies); 'for he is Lord of lords, and King of kings' the ground of victory (the Lamb conquering because he is supreme over all, his title, 19:16, marking his sovereign supremacy over all kings and powers, the kings and beast unable to prevail against the King of kings); 'they that are with him are called, and chosen, and faithful' the Lamb's followers (those who share his victory the called, chosen, and faithful, his elect and loyal people, the threefold description marking the true saints who accompany the Lamb and triumph with him) — Barnes, Robertson, Henry.

themes: these shall make war with the Lamb, and the Lamb shall overcome them
for he is Lord of lords, and King of kings: and they that are with him are called, and chosen, and faithful
the war with the Lamb and the Lamb's victory
the assured triumph of Christ over the antichristian powers

schools.summary: The waters as peoples: 'and he saith unto me, The waters which thou sawest, where the whore sitteth, are peoples, and multitudes, and nations, and tongues' — the 'many waters' (v.1) on which the harlot sits explained as peoples, multitudes, nations, and tongues (the whore enthroned over and ruling the masses of mankind, her dominion extending over the nations); 'the waters... are peoples, and multitudes, and nations, and tongues' the fourfold designation of the whole mass of mankind (the harlot's seat on the waters marking her rule and influence over all peoples, the universal extent of her corrupting sway); the waters as peoples showing that the harlot's power and seduction reach the multitudes of nations (her dominion over the populous world, the explanation confirming her as a great power ruling the peoples, consistent with v.1) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry.

themes: The waters which thou sawest, where the whore sitteth, are peoples, and multitudes, and nations, and tongues
the waters explained as peoples and nations
the harlot's dominion over the masses of mankind
the universal extent of her corrupting sway

schools.summary: The kings destroying the whore: 'and the ten horns which thou sawest upon the beast, these shall hate the whore, and shall make her desolate and naked, and shall eat her flesh, and burn her with fire' — the ten kings (and the beast) turning against the harlot they once supported (they hate her, strip her, devour her flesh, and burn her with fire, the powers that upheld Babylon becoming the instruments of her destruction); 'make her desolate and naked (strip her of her wealth and glory), and shall eat her flesh (devour her, ruin her utterly)' the total despoiling and destruction of the harlot by her former allies (the figure of a complete and violent overthrow); 'burn her with fire' (the harlot burned, as adulteresses and the condemned were burned, Leviticus 21:9, the burning of Babylon, 18:8, the fitting and final destruction of the corrupt city by the very powers that had served her, in the historicist reading the turning of the nations/kings against corrupt Rome) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, JFB.

themes: the ten horns which thou sawest upon the beast, these shall hate the whore
shall make her desolate and naked, and shall eat her flesh, and burn her with fire
the ten kings turning on and destroying the whore
the powers that upheld Babylon becoming the instruments of her destruction

schools.summary: God's overruling purpose: 'for God hath put in their hearts to fulfil his will, and to agree, and give their kingdom unto the beast, until the words of God shall be fulfilled' — the kings' turning on the harlot, their agreement, and their giving their kingdom to the beast all by God's overruling providence (God putting it in their hearts to fulfill his will, even the wicked powers unwittingly serving God's purpose until his words are accomplished); 'God hath put in their hearts to fulfil his will' the divine overruling (God so directing even the malice and agreement of the wicked kings that they accomplish his purpose, the destruction of Babylon, the sovereignty of God over the actions of his enemies, they fulfilling his will even in their wickedness); 'until the words of God shall be fulfilled' the appointed limit (the kings serving the beast until God's prophetic words are accomplished, God's purpose setting the term, when his words are fulfilled the antichristian powers having served their appointed end and themselves destroyed) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: God hath put in their hearts to fulfil his will, and to agree, and give their kingdom unto the beast
until the words of God shall be fulfilled
God's overruling purpose in the destruction of the whore
even the wicked powers unwittingly serving God's purpose

schools.summary: The woman as the great city: 'and the woman which thou sawest is that great city, which reigneth over the kings of the earth' — the angel concluding the explanation by identifying the woman (the harlot) as 'that great city' — Babylon (v.5), the great city that reigns over the kings of the earth (the harlot a city/power exercising dominion over the world's rulers); 'that great city' Babylon (16:19; 17:5), read by the historicists as Rome (papal Rome, the city on seven hills, v.9, the seat of the corrupt power that reigned over the kings), the preterist reading pagan Rome (the imperial city that ruled the world), the great city that dominates the earth's rulers; 'which reigneth over the kings of the earth' her dominion (she exercising sovereignty over the world's rulers, her dominion over the kings marking her as the great ruling power, Rome the mistress of nations, the harlot-city's rule over the kings of the earth now doomed to destruction) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: the woman which thou sawest is that great city, which reigneth over the kings of the earth
the woman identified as the great city reigning over the kings
Babylon (read as Rome), the great corrupt power dominating the world's rulers
the harlot-city doomed to destruction
